content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers 17 (NUM_017)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### Verse 2 -- Rendering of emor el... ve-yarem ("Say to... and let him lift up")
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Say unto Eleazar son of Aaron the priest, and-let-him-lift-up the fire-pans from-between the-burning, and-the-fire scatter yonder, for they-have-become-holy" |
| **LOGOS** | "Tell Eleazar son of Aaron the priest to pick up the censers from the midst of the burning, and scatter the fire far away, for they have become holy" |
| **PNEUMA** | *(did not translate this verse -- began chapter at MT v. 16)* |
| **SOPHIA** | "Say to Eleazar son of Aaron the priest that he shall pick up the censers from the midst of the burning, and scatter the fire far away, for they have become holy" |
| **Rationale** | EMET preserves the Hebrew syntax with "and-let-him-lift-up" (jussive) but produces stilted English. LOGOS smooths to an English infinitive construction "to pick up," which loses the imperative-then-jussive sequence of the Hebrew. The SOPHIA consensus uses "that he shall pick up" to preserve the directive force while remaining readable. "Censers" (LOGOS) is preferred over "fire-pans" (EMET) as the standard English term for makhtot in cultic context, though "fire-pans" is more literal. |

### Verse 2 -- "far away" vs. "yonder" (hal'ah)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "scatter yonder" |
| **LOGOS** | "scatter the fire far away" |
| **PNEUMA** | *(did not translate)* |
| **SOPHIA** | "scatter the fire far away" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew hal'ah means "further, yonder, far away." "Yonder" (EMET) is archaic but precise; "far away" (LOGOS) is more accessible. The consensus adopts "far away." |

### Verse 3 -- "at the cost of their lives" vs. "against their souls" (be-nafshosam)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "against-their-souls" |
| **LOGOS** | "at the cost of their lives" |
| **PNEUMA** | *(did not translate)* |
| **SOPHIA** | "at the cost of their lives" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew be-nafshosam (literally "by/with their souls/lives") carries instrumental or price force: their sin cost them their lives. EMET's "against their souls" preserves the preposition be- but may suggest the sin harmed their souls rather than that it cost their lives. LOGOS's "at the cost of their lives" captures the price-paying sense of the beth pretii. The consensus follows LOGOS. |

### Verse 3 -- "censers" vs. "fire-pans" (makhtot)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "fire-pans" |
| **LOGOS** | "censers" |
| **PNEUMA** | *(did not translate)* |
| **SOPHIA** | "censers" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew makhtot (from khatah, "to snatch up coals") refers to a vessel for carrying live coals. "Fire-pan" is the more literal rendering; "censer" is the established English liturgical term. The consensus uses "censers" for consistency with standard biblical English, while acknowledging that "fire-pans" more closely reflects the Hebrew etymology. |

### Verse 5 -- "like Korah and his congregation" vs. "like Korah and like his congregation"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "like-Korah and-like-his-congregation" |
| **LOGOS** | "like Korah and his congregation" |
| **PNEUMA** | *(did not translate)* |
| **SOPHIA** | "like Korah and like his congregation" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew ve-kha-adato has the comparative preposition ke- prefixed to adato. EMET preserves both prepositions; LOGOS drops the second. The SOPHIA consensus retains both to reflect the Hebrew precisely. |

### Verse 6 -- "murmured/grumbled" (va-yillonu)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-murmured all the-congregation" |
| **LOGOS** | "And the entire congregation... grumbled" |
| **PNEUMA** | *(did not translate)* |
| **SOPHIA** | "And the entire congregation... grumbled" |
| **Rationale** | Both "murmured" and "grumbled" are standard renderings of the niphal of lun. "Grumbled" carries a slightly more active, defiant connotation appropriate to the context of accusing Moses and Aaron of murder. The consensus uses "grumbled." |

### Verse 11 -- "fire-pan" vs. "censer" (makhtah)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Take the fire-pan" |
| **LOGOS** | "Take the censer" |
| **PNEUMA** | *(did not translate)* |
| **SOPHIA** | "Take the censer" |
| **Rationale** | Same term as vv. 2-3. Consensus uses "censer" throughout for consistency. |

### Verse 11 -- "go quickly" vs. "carry it quickly" (ve-holekh meherah)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-go quickly unto the-congregation" |
| **LOGOS** | "and carry it quickly to the congregation" |
| **PNEUMA** | *(did not translate)* |
| **SOPHIA** | "and carry it quickly to the congregation" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew ve-holekh meherah is literally "and go quickly." LOGOS adds "carry it" to make explicit what is implied (Aaron must carry the censer). The consensus follows LOGOS as the implied object clarifies the action, though EMET's literal rendering is defensible. |

### Verse 13 -- "was checked" vs. "was restrained" (va-te'atsar)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "was-restrained" |
| **LOGOS** | "was checked" |
| **PNEUMA** | *(did not translate)* |
| **SOPHIA** | "was checked" |
| **Rationale** | The niphal of atsar means "to be held back, restrained, stopped." Both renderings are faithful. "Checked" conveys the sudden halting of the plague's advance; "restrained" emphasizes the external force holding it back. The consensus uses "checked." |

### Verse 14 -- "on account of Korah" vs. "on the matter of Korah" (al devar Qorakh)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "on-the-matter of Korah" |
| **LOGOS** | "on account of Korah" |
| **PNEUMA** | *(did not translate)* |
| **SOPHIA** | "on account of Korah" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew al devar literally means "on the matter/word of." "On account of" (LOGOS) conveys the causal sense more naturally. "On the matter of" (EMET) is more literal but slightly awkward. The consensus follows LOGOS. |

### Verses 1-15 -- PNEUMA's Chapter Scope Disagreement
| Translator | Position |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | Translated all 28 verses of MT chapter 17 (vv. 1-28) |
| **LOGOS** | Translated all 28 verses of MT chapter 17 (vv. 1-28) |
| **PNEUMA** | Began chapter 17 at MT v. 16 (= English 17:1), translating only 13 verses (MT vv. 16-28), and continued into chapter 18 material (priestly duties, Levitical gifts) |
| **SOPHIA** | Follows the Masoretic versification: 28 verses |
| **Rationale** | The Leningrad Codex clearly marks the parashah division at what the MT numbers as 17:1. PNEUMA followed the English Bible convention of beginning chapter 17 at what the MT numbers as v. 16. The SOPHIA consensus follows the MT chapter division as the primary source text is the Leningrad Codex. PNEUMA's rendering of the material from MT 17:16-28 is incorporated into the consensus for those verses. |

### Verse 20 -- "shall bud" vs. "will sprout" (yifrakh)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "will-bud" |
| **LOGOS** | "shall bud" |
| **PNEUMA** | "will sprout" |
| **SOPHIA** | "shall bud" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew yifrakh (qal imperfect of parakh) means "to bud, sprout, blossom." PNEUMA's "sprout" is broader; EMET and LOGOS both use "bud," which more precisely matches parakh and connects to the fulfillment in v. 23. "Shall" (LOGOS) conveys the promissory force of the divine declaration better than "will." |

### Verse 20 -- "cause to cease" vs. "silence" vs. "cause to subside" (va-hashikkoti)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-I-will-cause-to-subside from-upon-me the-murmurings" |
| **LOGOS** | "I will cause to cease from upon me the grumblings" |
| **PNEUMA** | "I will silence against myself the grumblings" |
| **SOPHIA** | "I will cause to cease from upon me the grumblings" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew va-hashikkoti (hifil of shakhakh, "to subside, abate") means to cause something to die down or cease. PNEUMA's "silence against myself" is vivid and captures the relational force of me-alai ("from upon me") but introduces a reflexive element not in the Hebrew verb. EMET's "cause to subside" is closest to the root meaning. LOGOS's "cause to cease" is slightly more decisive. The consensus follows LOGOS, retaining the crucial "from upon me" that conveys YHWH's experience of the complaints as a burden. |

### Verse 21 -- "one staff for each leader" vs. "a staff for one leader, a staff for one leader"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "a-rod for-one leader, a-rod for-one leader, for-the-house of their-fathers" |
| **LOGOS** | "one staff for each leader, according to their fathers' houses" |
| **PNEUMA** | "one staff for each leader according to their ancestral houses" |
| **SOPHIA** | "one staff for each leader, according to their fathers' houses" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew repeats matteh le-ekhad nasi matteh le-ekhad nasi (distributive doubling). EMET preserves the full repetition. LOGOS and PNEUMA condense to "one staff for each leader." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA for readability, as the distributive sense is clear without the verbatim doubling, and the doubling in v. 17 (matteh matteh) is already preserved. |

### Verse 21 -- "fathers' houses" vs. "ancestral houses"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "father's house" / "their-fathers" |
| **LOGOS** | "fathers' houses" |
| **PNEUMA** | "ancestral houses" |
| **SOPHIA** | "fathers' houses" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew beit avotam is literally "house of their fathers." PNEUMA's "ancestral houses" is an interpretive smoothing that loses the patriarchal terminology. EMET and LOGOS preserve the literal rendering. The consensus uses "fathers' houses." |

### Verse 23 -- "budded" vs. "sprouted" (parakh)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "had-budded" |
| **LOGOS** | "had budded" |
| **PNEUMA** | "had sprouted" |
| **SOPHIA** | "had budded" |
| **Rationale** | Same root parakh as in v. 20. PNEUMA uses "sprouted" which is broader in meaning. EMET and LOGOS use "budded" which maintains the verbal link to YHWH's prediction in v. 20 ("his staff shall bud"). Consistency demands "budded." |

### Verse 25 -- "sons of rebellion" vs. "the rebellious" (benei meri)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "for-the-sons of rebellion" |
| **LOGOS** | "for the sons of rebellion" |
| **PNEUMA** | "for the rebellious" |
| **SOPHIA** | "for the sons of rebellion" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew benei meri is literally "sons of rebellion" -- a characteristic Semitic idiom describing those defined by a quality. PNEUMA's "the rebellious" conveys the meaning but loses the Hebrew idiom. EMET and LOGOS preserve the literal phrase. The consensus retains "sons of rebellion." |

### Verse 25 -- "put an end to" vs. "put an end, an utter end, to" (u-tekhol)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-you-shall-finish their-murmurings from-upon-me" |
| **LOGOS** | "that you may put an end to their grumblings against me" |
| **PNEUMA** | "so that you may put an end, an utter end, to their grumblings against me" |
| **SOPHIA** | "that you may put an end to their grumblings against me" |
| **Rationale** | PNEUMA doubled the phrase ("put an end, an utter end") to convey intensity, but the Hebrew u-tekhol is a single piel imperfect of kalah without an infinitive absolute construction. The doubling PNEUMA introduces is not warranted by the Hebrew grammar here. LOGOS's straightforward rendering is more accurate. |

### Verse 27 -- "we perish" vs. "we are perishing" vs. "we have expired" (gavanu)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "we-have-expired, we-have-perished, all-of-us have-perished" |
| **LOGOS** | "We perish! We are lost! All of us are lost!" |
| **PNEUMA** | "We are perishing! We are lost! All of us are lost!" |
| **SOPHIA** | "We perish! We are lost! All of us are lost!" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew uses qal perfects (gavanu, avadnu) which can express the certainty of a present/impending condition ("we are as good as dead"). EMET's "have expired" treats them as completed actions. LOGOS's present tense ("We perish!") captures the exclamatory, present-tense force of the prophetic/dramatic perfect. PNEUMA's progressive "We are perishing!" adds duration not present in the perfects. The consensus follows LOGOS. |

### Verse 28 -- "Shall we ever cease perishing?" vs. "Have we finished dying?" vs. "Are we to be completely finished off by dying?"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Have-we-finished dying?" |
| **LOGOS** | "Shall we ever cease perishing?" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Are we to be completely finished off by dying?" |
| **SOPHIA** | "Shall we ever cease perishing?" |
| **Rationale** | Hebrew ha-im tamnu ligvo'a ("have we been completed/finished to die?") is a desperate rhetorical question. EMET is most literal ("Have we finished dying?") but could be misread as asking whether the dying is over. LOGOS captures the existential dread: the question implies the dying may never end. PNEUMA introduces "completely finished off" which adds intensity beyond the Hebrew. The consensus follows LOGOS as the most effective rendering of the underlying despair. |

### Verse 28 -- "Tabernacle" vs. "tabernacle" (mishkan)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "the-Tabernacle of YHWH" |
| **LOGOS** | "the Tabernacle of YHWH" |
| **PNEUMA** | "the tabernacle of YHWH" |
| **SOPHIA** | "the Tabernacle of YHWH" |
| **Rationale** | The Hebrew mishkan YHWH refers to the specific dwelling-place of YHWH in the wilderness. Capitalization as a proper noun ("Tabernacle") is appropriate to distinguish this unique sacred structure. EMET and LOGOS capitalize; PNEUMA does not. The consensus capitalizes. |

---

*-- SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter*
